title: Improved Fault-Tolerant Non-Clifford Gates (or: How to Multiply Quantumly)
speaker: Louis Golowich
abstract:
A principal challenge in realizing the potential of quantum computing lies in our ability to perform computations fault-tolerantly, in the presence of the noise inherent to quantum devices. Non-Clifford quantum gates, which are analogous to the classical multiplication (i.e. AND) gate, are particularly difficult to implement fault-tolerantly. We show how to perform such gates with significantly lower asymptotic overhead than was achievable with prior techniques.
For this purpose, we present new constructions of quantum error-correcting codes supporting transversal non-Clifford gates, meaning that the desired logical gates can be executed by applying a constant-depth physical circuit to the code state. In particular, we present the first asymptotically good such codes, as well as the first such codes with low-weight parity-checks that have almost linear dimension and polynomial distance. Our constructions are based on algebraic codes and expander graphs, which we combine using topological techniques.
No prior knowledge of quantum computing will be assumed.
Based on joint works with Venkatesan Guruswami and Ting-Chun Lin.

proof_stages.label: Fault-tolerance target and transversal-gate paradigm
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames the problem as reducing physical error rates by encoded, layer-by-layer computation, where transversal implementations keep errors from spreading too far before correction.

proof_stages.label: CSS-code and CCZ/multiplication dictionary
proof_stages.summary: Classical linear codes, CSS coset quantum codes, transversal classical multiplication, and the CCZ phase gate are set up so that non-Clifford fault-tolerant gates can be studied via multiplicative code structure.

proof_stages.label: Asymptotically good non-LDPC construction
proof_stages.summary: A classical code family whose code, dual, and triple-product code are asymptotically good yields asymptotically good CSS codes with transversal CCZ, and algebraic-geometry codes give the desired constant-alphabet family and low magic-state-distillation overhead.

proof_stages.label: Local-check requirement and LDPC target theorem
proof_stages.summary: Because the error-correction procedure itself must tolerate faults, the speaker motivates quantum LDPC or nearly LDPC codes, explains the CSS degeneracy obstruction to proving distance, and states the improved nearly LDPC transversal-CCZ parameters.

proof_stages.label: Sparse classical multiplicative LDPC input
proof_stages.summary: Starting from Reed-Muller multiplication, the proof constructs a sparse affine-line expander and a Tanner code with punctured Reed-Solomon local constraints so that the global classical code and its transpose have good distance while retaining embedded multiplicative Reed-Muller codewords.

proof_stages.label: Three-dimensional quantum product code
proof_stages.summary: A three-dimensional hypergraph/topological product of the classical LDPC input gives the nearly LDPC quantum code, with roughly preserved rate, distance about n^{1/3}, and a product dimension matched to the cubic nature of CCZ.

proof_stages.label: Transversal CCZ on the product code
proof_stages.summary: The product code is interpreted as a cubical complex whose local multiplication-compatible pieces glue together so that physical transversal CCZ induces disjoint logical CCZ gates on the encoded qubits.

proof_stages.label: Grassmannian-expander reformulation and future route
proof_stages.summary: The final stage relates multiplicative code constructions to line-based Grassmannian-type expanders and high-dimensional expanders, suggesting that better such expanders would improve both classical and quantum LDPC transversal-gate codes.

examples.name: RSA-scale target computation
examples.note: The mention of running about 10^10--10^15 gates to attack RSA illustrates the size of the reliability gap rather than producing a proof idea.
examples.engagement_reason: RSA is only cited as a target application requiring about 10^10–10^15 gates, with no computation carried out on an RSA instance.

passes.reason: An RSA-scale/Shor-style computation is used as an ordinary large target workload motivating fault tolerance, not as a structural exception or refutation.

examples.name: Quantum repetition-code benchmark
examples.note: The quantum analogue of a classical repetition code is used as a removable benchmark for previous transversal-CCZ LDPC constructions, not as a component of the new proof.
examples.engagement_reason: The quantum repetition-code benchmark is used as prior-state-of-the-art comparison with stated parameters, but no construction or calculation is performed on it.

passes.reason: The quantum repetition-code analogue has essentially constant logical dimension/zero asymptotic rate and serves only as the prior benchmark baseline.
The quantum repetition-code benchmark has collapsed constant logical dimension and the highly regular repeated-code structure, serving only as a baseline.
The repetition-code analogue is a rate-zero constant-message, maximally redundant boundary benchmark rather than a counterexample.
the repetition-code baseline is a minimal-rate repetition-style code sitting at an extreme simple benchmark rather than an adversarial example
A repetition-code benchmark is the highly low-rate/minimal-message baseline, with the classical repetition code attaining the maximal-distance endpoint for its one-dimensional code family.
a repetition-type benchmark is a rate-zero constant-logical boundary code family rather than a refutation
The repetition-code benchmark is a boundary low-rate repetition-type construction, essentially maximal-distance/minimal-information in spirit.
The repetition-code benchmark is the highly redundant zero-rate baseline, extremal in the repetition-code sense of trading essentially all rate for distance.
Used as the prior baseline to beat; a repetition-type quantum code is a maximally redundant, constant-rate-zero boundary case.

examples.name: Two-error pattern under transversal gates
examples.note: A small visual pattern of errors in two physical qubits shows concretely why transversal gates keep errors confined to columns.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker traces a concrete two-error pattern through transversal gates, arguing the errors remain within the same columns and do not spread between code blocks.

passes.reason: The two-error pattern is only a small illustrative low-weight noise instance showing limited propagation under transversal gates.

examples.name: Decoherence as low-weight random noise
examples.note: In response to a question, decoherence is compared to independent random errors whose expansion is dominated by low-weight terms with high probability, clarifying the error model.
examples.engagement_reason: In response to a question, the speaker analyzes decoherence by expanding it as independent small-probability errors and reasoning that with high probability it gives low-weight noise.

passes.reason: Decoherence is modeled as standard independent low-probability local noise that is low-weight with high probability, used to justify the error model.
Decoherence is modeled as small-probability, hence typically low-weight, random noise supporting the standard error model.
Decoherence is modeled as a distribution concentrated on sparse low-weight error terms, not as an adversarial failure case.
a standard stochastic low-weight noise interpretation invoked to justify the local-error model
Decoherence is modeled as standard stochastic local noise whose expansion is likely low-weight, with no sporadic or optimal structure.
standard stochastic low-probability noise used to justify the localized low-weight error model
Decoherence is modeled as ordinary stochastic low-rate noise to justify the error model, not as a special or failing case.
Decoherence is modeled as ordinary stochastic low-probability local noise with high-probability low weight, supporting the error model rather than challenging it.
Used to justify modeling decoherence as stochastic low-weight noise, a standard noise picture rather than a structurally special obstruction.
decoherence is modeled as noise whose likely expansion terms are sparse low-weight errors, supporting the noise model
Decoherence is treated as the enemy noise process that the error-correction model must tolerate, modeled by typical low-weight stochastic errors.
Decoherence is modeled as ordinary small-probability product noise that is typically low weight, used to justify the error model.

examples.name: Controlled-Z/CNOT as the two-input case
examples.note: The two-input controlled phase is explained as a Clifford operation equivalent to CNOT after a Hadamard change of basis, illustrating why the talk focuses on the three-input CCZ gate.
examples.engagement_reason: The two-input case is examined by relating CZ via a Hadamard basis change to CNOT and describing its action as mapping (a,b) to (a,a+b).

passes.reason: The two-input controlled-phase case collapses to a Clifford gate, locally equivalent to CNOT, making it a lower-degree base case rather than the hard non-Clifford case.
The two-input controlled-Z case collapses to a Clifford/CNOT-type gate, a lower-complexity base case of the non-Clifford CCZ discussion.
CZ/CNOT is the lower-arity Clifford base case of the controlled-phase hierarchy, used to contrast with genuinely non-Clifford CCZ.

The two-input CZ/CNOT case is a lower-arity/base case that collapses to a Clifford/addition-type gate rather than the non-Clifford CCZ behavior.

The two-input CZ/CNOT case is a collapsed lower-arity case where the non-Clifford difficulty disappears into a Clifford gate.
